questions.question: What happens when a UDP packet is lost in transit?
options: The kernel automatically retransmits it
The sender gets an error from sendto()
Nothing — the sender never knows unless the application checks
The receiver sends a NAK (negative acknowledgement)
questions.explanation: UDP has no delivery confirmation. sendto() returns successfully as soon as the kernel hands the datagram to the network interface — it has no idea whether it arrived. If you need to know about loss, your application must implement its own acknowledgement scheme.

questions.question: Why do DNS queries use UDP instead of TCP?
options: DNS requires message boundaries that only UDP preserves
UDP queries are smaller than TCP because there's no header
DNS queries are short and latency matters — no need for connection overhead
TCP cannot handle the volume of DNS traffic on the internet
questions.explanation: A DNS query/response round trip is typically under 512 bytes. Setting up a TCP connection (3-way handshake) adds a full RTT of latency before the query is even sent. For a one-shot question-and-answer exchange, UDP's lower overhead wins. If the response is too large, DNS falls back to TCP.

questions.question: What does SOCK_DGRAM tell the OS when creating a socket?
options: Use the TCP transport protocol
Use the UDP transport protocol (datagram mode)
Create a raw socket that bypasses the transport layer
Create a Unix domain socket for IPC
questions.explanation: SOCK_DGRAM selects UDP (datagram-oriented) semantics. SOCK_STREAM selects TCP (stream-oriented). You combine this with AF_INET (IPv4) or AF_INET6 (IPv6) to get the full socket type: socket(AF_INET, SOCK_DGRAM) = a UDP/IPv4 socket.

questions.question: Why does the UDP echo server use recvfrom() instead of recv()?
options: recvfrom() is required for UDP; recv() only works on TCP
recvfrom() is faster for large datagrams
recvfrom() returns the sender's address, needed to send the reply
recv() would block forever on a UDP socket
questions.explanation: Unlike TCP, a UDP server has no persistent connection, so it doesn't know who sent the datagram from the socket state alone. recvfrom() returns (data, sender_address), which the server uses in sendto(data, sender_address) to reply. You could also use recv() if the server doesn't need to reply.

questions.question: In the UDP client, why is the send timestamp packed into the payload instead of recorded in a separate variable?
options: struct.pack() is more accurate than time.time()
So the server can measure the RTT on its side
So the RTT measurement works even if replies arrive out of order
To reduce the number of system calls
questions.explanation: UDP datagrams can arrive out of order. If seq=5 arrives before seq=4, a simple 'record send time in a list' approach would match the wrong timestamp. Embedding the timestamp in the payload means each reply carries its own t_send, so RTT = t_recv - payload.t_send regardless of arrival order.
